Frequency Timing

The Full Annual Window Cleaning Schedule for BC Homeowners

Published 2026-05-17 · Wagon Windows

There's no single right answer to how often to clean windows in BC — it depends on your property type, location, sun exposure, and what you're trying to protect. This guide builds a full annual schedule by property type, so you're not over-cleaning what doesn't need it or under-cleaning what does. The framework is built for Shuswap-region homeowners.

Need professional window cleaning in the Shuswap?

Book Online — Free Quote (403) 837-5092

Why a Schedule Matters More Than a Single Clean

Most homeowners in BC treat window cleaning as a reactive task — they notice the windows look dirty and book a clean. The problem with reactive cleaning is that it's always slightly too late. Mineral deposits that have been on glass for more than a season become progressively harder to remove. Organic residue — pollen, smoke film, insect material — bonds to glass under UV and heat faster than it looks. By the time windows look noticeably dirty, some of that residue has already begun the chemical process of etching into the glass surface. A proactive schedule prevents the worst outcomes while reducing the total cleaning effort over time. Clean glass accumulates deposits more slowly than glass with a layer of residue that acts as a bonding surface for new material. Regular cleaning is, counterintuitively, less work than sporadic deep cleaning. The schedule below isn't a sales pitch for maximum service frequency. It's calibrated to what the BC interior climate actually demands by property type, based on the environmental factors that drive deposit accumulation in this region.

Property Type 1: Standard Residential — Twice Per Year

For a standard Salmon Arm or Shuswap residential property — single or two-story home, city or suburban lot, no lakefront or agricultural adjacency — twice per year is the right baseline. Spring clean (late April to mid-May): Remove winter mineral buildup, spring pollen, and any freeze-thaw residue. This is the priority clean of the year — don't skip it. Fall clean (mid-September to mid-October): Remove summer UV-baked residue, any wildfire smoke film, and summer hard water accumulation before it goes through winter freeze-thaw cycles. Between those two cleans, interior glass can be wiped down as needed — this is a DIY job that takes an hour and doesn't require professional equipment. The exterior twice-a-year schedule handles the accumulation that DIY tools can't address. For properties with significant tree coverage (cottonwoods, birch, or conifers dropping sap), add a third clean in mid-summer to address sap and early organic buildup before heat bakes it on. Cottonwood fluff season in the Shuswap in late May and June is particularly aggressive — properties with nearby cottonwoods often benefit from a June touch-up.

  • Spring (late April–mid-May): Priority clean — mineral buildup, pollen, winter residue
  • Fall (mid-September–mid-October): UV-baked residue, smoke film, summer deposits
  • Optional summer clean for properties with heavy cottonwood or conifer coverage

Property Type 2: Lakefront and Waterfront Properties — Three Times Per Year

Lakefront properties on Shuswap Lake, Mara Lake, or any of the region's smaller lakes face a fundamentally different exposure profile than standard residential properties. Water aerosols from lake surface evaporation carry dissolved minerals that deposit on glass continuously through the warm months. Boat traffic and wave action increase aerosol production. The south-facing glass on lakefront homes faces maximum UV exposure combined with maximum mineral aerosol load. For lakefront homes, three cleans per year is the practical minimum: Spring (late April–May): Post-winter reset, before the lake season begins. Mid-summer (late June–July): Addresses the first wave of lake mineral deposits and any spring pollen that wasn't fully removed. This is the clean that protects the glass going into the peak summer heat when baking-on is fastest. Fall (September): Pre-winter removal of a full summer's mineral, UV, and boat-spray accumulation. Skipping the mid-summer clean on a lakefront property means going into fall with three months of baked-on lake mineral deposits. Those are significantly harder to remove than deposits that have only been on glass for six weeks. Hard water stain treatment on lakefront windows is substantially more involved than on standard residential glass.

  • Spring (April–May): Post-winter reset before lake season
  • Mid-summer (June–July): First wave mineral deposits, pre-peak-heat protection
  • Fall (September): Full summer accumulation removed before winter

Property Type 3: Rural and Agricultural-Adjacent Properties

Rural properties in the Shuswap valley — particularly those near agricultural operations, orchards, or vineyards — face specific contamination sources that standard residential schedules don't account for. Agricultural spray drift during spring and summer application seasons deposits chemical residue on glass that can be corrosive to frames and seals if left in place. Dust from unpaved roads and agricultural traffic creates an abrasive film that, when wet, acts like a mild sandpaper on glass surfaces. For rural properties, the scheduling considerations are: Post-spray-season clean (early June in most Shuswap agricultural areas): Removes any spring application drift before heat bakes it on. Fall clean (September): Standard pre-winter clean, now more important because of accumulated dust and chemical residue from the growing season. Harvest-dust clean (optional, October–early November depending on crop type): Orchard and vineyard operations generate significant dust during harvest. A post-harvest clean in October protects windows heading into winter. Rural properties also tend to have more hard water risk from well water irrigation — check for mineral deposits quarterly rather than annually.

Adding Gutter Cleaning to the Schedule

Gutters aren't technically window cleaning, but they belong on the same annual schedule because they directly affect window cleanliness. Overflowing or debris-clogged gutters flush mineral-laden water down over window frames and glass. No window cleaning schedule is complete without gutter maintenance baked in. For standard residential properties: one gutter clean per year in late October or early November, after peak leaf fall. In the Shuswap, this typically means after the cottonwoods and maples have dropped. For properties with heavy conifer coverage (pine or spruce needle drop is year-round): two gutter cleans per year — spring and fall. For properties with irrigation systems that use roof-mounted or elevated distribution: check gutters after each growing season for mineral buildup from irrigation overspray. Gutter cleaning in Salmon Arm starts at $149. Combining gutter and window service visits reduces the per-visit cost and ensures the sequencing is right — gutters before windows.

The Schedule in Summary

A clean summary of the annual schedule by property type for Shuswap-region homeowners: Standard residential: Spring (April–May) + Fall (September–October) = 2 exterior cleans per year. Lakefront or waterfront: Spring + Mid-summer + Fall = 3 exterior cleans per year. Rural/agricultural: Spring + Post-spray-season + Fall (+ optional harvest clean) = 2–4 cleans per year depending on operation proximity. Gutters (all property types): 1–2 times per year, timed to leaf fall and growing season. Interior glass: DIY as needed between professional exterior cleans — typically 2–4 times per year depending on household habits. For Shuswap-area homeowners looking to book, call (403) 837-5092 or visit wagonwindow.com. Residential window cleaning starts at $169, gutter cleaning from $149.

  • Standard residential: 2x/year (spring + fall)
  • Lakefront/waterfront: 3x/year (spring, mid-summer, fall)
  • Rural/agricultural: 2–4x/year depending on spray season and harvest
  • Gutters: 1–2x/year — always before, not after, window cleans

Frequently Asked Questions

How often should I clean windows in BC compared to other provinces?

BC homeowners in the interior face a more aggressive window-contamination environment than most of Canada. The combination of wildfire smoke seasons (increasingly common in the Okanagan and Shuswap region), hard water from glacial and snowmelt-fed water systems, high UV exposure in summer, and freeze-thaw mineral cycling in winter creates a higher deposit rate than, say, Ontario or the Maritimes. Standard advice for most of Canada is once or twice a year. In the BC interior — particularly in the Shuswap, Okanagan, and Kamloops corridor — twice a year is the baseline minimum, and three times per year is appropriate for lakefront or high-exposure properties. The high mineral content of BC interior water supplies is the biggest differentiating factor. Hard water deposits require more frequent treatment here than in lower-mineral regions.

Can I just clean my windows once a year?

For some properties, yes — once a year is enough. Ground-floor windows with good overhangs, minimal sun exposure, no hard water irrigation impact, and no nearby agricultural or wildfire smoke exposure can often be maintained with a single annual clean. The risk with once-a-year cleaning is timing: if you do only one clean and it's in spring, you go into fall and winter with a full summer of UV-baked residue that will set harder over winter. If you do only one clean and it's in fall, you go through the entire summer with winter residue baked on in the heat. The one-clean approach works best if you're flexible about the exact timing each year — choosing based on what the season has actually deposited rather than a fixed calendar date. If you can only do one, late September is the best single timing in the Shuswap: you've caught summer deposits before winter sets them, and the glass goes into freeze-thaw season as clean as possible.

Do second-story windows need cleaning as often as ground floor?

Second-story and higher windows actually accumulate certain types of deposits faster than ground-floor windows, despite the intuition that they're less exposed. Upper-story windows receive more direct UV exposure (less shading from landscaping), more wind-driven dust and pollen, and more direct contact with roof runoff on windows positioned below roof edges or gutters. In the Shuswap, upper-story windows on the south and west elevations often show advanced mineral and UV-baked deposits faster than ground-floor north-facing glass on the same house. The frequency recommendation doesn't change by story — twice a year for standard residential applies to all floors. What changes is the method: upper-story windows require ladders or water-fed poles, which is why professional cleaning is particularly worth the cost for second-story and above.

What happens if I skip the fall window clean?

Skipping the fall clean means going into BC winter with a full summer of UV-baked residue on your exterior glass. During winter, freeze-thaw cycles draw moisture into and out of that residue layer repeatedly — each cycle pushing mineral particles slightly deeper into micro-surface irregularities in the glass. Hard water deposits that are still chemically removable in fall become progressively harder to remove as they go through winter. Come spring, you're not just cleaning a summer's worth of deposits — you're cleaning deposits that have had five months of freeze-thaw bonding. Professional hard water stain treatment starts at $149 and is needed when standard cleaning can no longer address the residue. Skipping the fall clean one year occasionally doesn't create a crisis. Skipping it every year builds up a progressively harder and more expensive remediation problem.
